title: Why You Shouldn't Blow Up Abortion Clinics
author: Nathan Weisser
orientation: Nathan Weisser argues that vigilante violence against abortion clinics and abortionists is itself a form of incrementalism that targets methods rather than the root cause of abortion, sidelines effective abolitionist labor, and produces vainglory rather than rescued children. He grounds the argument in 2 Corinthians 10, the example of repentant mothers, and the long-term failure of the Rescue movement and the Army of God.
audiences: Christian sympathizers tempted to celebrate clinic bombers
Pro-life activists with a clinic-centric strategy
Abolitionists who need to articulate why they reject vigilantism
Skeptical onlookers accusing abolitionists of cowardice
Pastors and theologians weighing the ethics of the sword

questions.question: What did Roxanne Green's clinic arson actually accomplish, in Weisser's assessment?
questions.audience: Christian sympathizers tempted to celebrate clinic bombers
questions.answer: It produced a viral mugshot and five years in prison, while the clinic she burned simply reopened a year later and continues to kill children, leaving her absent from the actual rescue work she could have been doing.
questions.quote: the only thing she actually achieved was putting herself behind bars and wasting 5 years of her young and potentially productive life. The clinic she torched in 2022 merely delayed their opening by a year, where it stands today actively killing children while she's helpless – behind bars.

questions.question: How does Weisser respond to the charge that abolitionists are cowards unwilling to 'get their hands dirty'?
questions.audience: Skeptical onlookers accusing abolitionists of cowardice
questions.answer: He flips the framing: vigilantism is not bold action but a worthless incremental strategy, while abolitionists pursue the harder work of immediatism that calls the entire culture to repent rather than chasing a single method of killing.
questions.quote: What they don't understand is that we are *immediatists*, and we reject incremental strategies that delay the progress towards national repentance. Vigilantism is just that: a worthless incremental strategy.

questions.question: What scriptural argument does Weisser use to ground the rejection of vigilante violence?
questions.audience: Pastors and theologians weighing the ethics of the sword
questions.answer: He appeals to 2 Corinthians 10:4-6, where Paul says the weapons of Christian warfare are not carnal but have divine power to destroy arguments and lofty opinions, with prophetic speech as the prescribed instrument of punishing disobedience.
questions.quote: *…We destroy arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, and take every thought captive to obey Christ, being ready to punish every disobedience, when your obedience is complete.* (2 Corinthians 10:5-6 ESV)

questions.question: Does Weisser claim vigilantism is always inherently sinful?
questions.audience: Pastors and theologians weighing the ethics of the sword
questions.answer: No. He distinguishes his position from pacifism and concedes there are conceivable cases like Hitler or Jael and Sisera, but argues the abortion situation is not analogous and that Romans 13 normatively assigns the sword to the governing authorities.
questions.quote: My argument is not necessarily that vigilantism is inherently unjust, but that in this specific context, it's wrongheaded, and incrementalist in nature.

questions.question: Why does Weisser say bombing clinics is structurally identical to incremental pro-life bills?
questions.audience: Pro-life activists with a clinic-centric strategy
questions.answer: Because both attack a single method of abortion rather than the root demand for abortion itself, leaving every other method intact and conceding the basic legitimacy of the practice.
questions.quote: If we tried to burn down abortion mills, we would be merely going after one of those methods, just like pro-lifers do when they try and ban forceps abortions, but leave many other methods legal.

questions.question: What case does Weisser make about George Tiller's assassination?
questions.audience: Christian sympathizers tempted to celebrate clinic bombers
questions.answer: Tiller was reportedly about to lose his license for illegal activity, but Scott Roeder's killing turned a soon-to-be-disgraced abortionist into a pro-abortion martyr, and the women he would have aborted simply went elsewhere.
questions.quote: George Tiller, the abortionist assassinated by Scott Roeder, for example, was reportedly about to have his license revoked for his illegal activity, but then right before that happened he was killed by a pro-lifer and he became a pro-abortion martyr instead. Then, all those babies that were supposedly "saved" were just murdered some other way.

questions.question: What does Weisser say went wrong with the Rescue movement?
questions.audience: Pro-life activists with a clinic-centric strategy
questions.answer: Its clinic-centric tactics like blocking doors and bike-locking entrances jailed good fathers and emptied churches, while the clinics resumed business as usual the moment the activists were removed.
questions.quote: They would peacefully sit outside of abortion clinics and block people from going in, or they would bike lock the doors shut, etc. What would happen is they would just get arrested and the clinic would continue operating as normal as soon as they were out of the way.

questions.question: How does Weisser use his own state as evidence against the clinic-bombing strategy?
questions.audience: Pro-life activists with a clinic-centric strategy
questions.answer: He notes that he lives in a state with no operating clinics and yet, because pro-lifers settled for clinic-centric wins, abortion has actually increased through other channels, proving incrementalism just teaches the other side to be more efficient.
questions.quote: I, for one, live in a state with no clinics operating, so I get to live somewhere where the "good" the clinic bombers were trying to achieve HAS ALREADY been achieved! There's no abortionists, there's no abortion clinics. But because pro-lifers had this short-sighted clinic-centric mindset, there's now MORE abortion than there was previously.

questions.question: Why does Weisser insist mothers, not abortionists, are the principal actors in abortion?
questions.audience: Abolitionists who need to articulate why they reject vigilantism
questions.answer: Because abortionists are merely hitmen meeting demand; the woman who hires the abortion is the one committing murder, which is why pro-lifers' instinct to make the doctor the villain misidentifies the real perpetrator.
questions.quote: The people who are perpetuating the genocide of abortion are not the abortionists, they're the mothers. Abortionists are merely meeting a demand. Pro-lifers have always refused to see it that way, which is why it's understandable when they see the abortionists as the villain that needs to be stopped.

questions.question: What concrete personal example does Weisser offer to show the long-term cost of vigilantism?
questions.audience: Abolitionists who need to articulate why they reject vigilantism
questions.answer: A friend whose daughter aborted his grandchild chose prayer and gospel preaching over killing the abortionist Larry Burns; the daughter became a Christian abolitionist, and the friend now lobbies Oklahoma legislators rather than rotting in a prison cell.
questions.quote: Imagine instead, if he had killed Larry Burns, the abortionist. This man wouldn't be around now to be the incredible help that he is to our organization, because he'd be in jail. Plus, all the babies he and his daughter have had a hand in saving since then would currently be dead.

questions.question: What metaphor does Weisser use to describe vigilantism's relationship to the abortion industry?
questions.audience: Skeptical onlookers accusing abolitionists of cowardice
questions.answer: He likens it to chopping the tail off a dragon: the visible appendage is severed, the activist feels heroic, but the beast itself regenerates and the underlying system is untouched.
questions.quote: Vigilantism is a form of incrementalism, because it eschews the hard work of abolition in favor of something easier and more accessible. It's like chopping the tail off of a dragon. It just grows back.

questions.question: Whose work does Weisser hold up as the real model of abolitionist faithfulness?
questions.audience: Abolitionists who need to articulate why they reject vigilantism
questions.answer: The unglamorous blue-collar Christian who emails legislators, donates, talks honestly with co-workers, attends rallies, and stands at the mill, performing the boring repeated faithfulness no one applauds online.
questions.quote: The blue-collar worker who spends his free time emailing his legislators, giving his hard-earned money to good organizations, and having hard conversations with his co-workers about abortion. How about the guy who joins his church going to rallies for abolition, or standing outside of abortion mills trying to save lives? There's very little glory sent their way.

questions.question: What spiritual diagnosis does Weisser offer of the Army of God admirers?
questions.audience: Christian sympathizers tempted to celebrate clinic bombers
questions.answer: He sees vainglory: the appeal of public mugshots and dramatic displays gives trolls a cheap counter to abolitionists' call to repent of apathy, while accomplishing nothing for the children.
questions.quote: I think there's a sort of vainglory that comes along with the Army of God folks, the Rescue movement to a lesser degree, and a lot of these chronically-online trolls who prop up vigilantes who have "done what needed to be done."

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Defines the immediatist commitment that Weisser appeals to as the alternative to vigilante incrementalism.

related.title: Abolitionists Must Stand Firm to Oppose Murder, Love Murderers
related.reason: Develops the parallel claim that mothers, not abortionists, are the principal actors who must be confronted in love.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Lays out the lawful, magistrate-driven path to ending abortion that Weisser contrasts with vigilante violence.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Frames the broader critique of pro-life clinic-centric strategy that Weisser extends to clinic bombing.

related.title: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Reinforces the patient, prayerful posture Weisser commends over impatience with God's timing.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Provides the doctrinal contrast Weisser invokes when calling vigilantism a species of incrementalism.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Catalogs the strategic failures of method-by-method pro-life tactics that parallel the failures of clinic bombing.
